comments:
Summary:
The paper addresses AllSAT (enumerating all satisfying assignments) by proposing a simple extension of CDCL SAT solvers called BR-Enum (Backtrack with Reason). After the first model is found, the solver disables restarts and, for a branch corresponding to a model with decision literals <x1,...,xn>, backtracks to level n-1 and forces propagation of the negation of the last decision using the clause (¬x1 ∨ ... ∨ ¬xn). The motivation is to avoid the large overhead of adding explicit blocking clauses for each model. The authors report experimental comparisons (on SAT Challenge instances and SAT encodings of sequence mining) showing BR-Enum finds many more models and runs faster than a blocking-clause based approach implemented on MiniSAT 2.2.

Strengths:
- Tackles an important practical problem: efficient enumeration of models (AllSAT) which has applications in data mining and other areas.
- The proposed method is simple and easy to implement on top of an existing CDCL solver.
- Empirical results are suggestive that the approach can outperform naive blocking-clause enumeration on several benchmarks, including domain-encoded problems (sequence mining).

Weaknesses and issues to address:
1) Formal correctness and completeness are not clearly established. The manuscript does not provide a formal proof or sufficient argument that BR-Enum will enumerate all models exactly once and terminate (given the modifications: no restarts, using a ‘‘reason’’ clause to force alternative propagation). It is unclear whether learned clauses, non-chronological backtracking, and other CDCL behaviors might cause missed models or duplicates. A correctness argument (or precise conditions under which correctness holds) is necessary.

2) Algorithm description is too informal. The pseudo-code is missing; the mechanism of deriving and using the clause (¬x1 ∨ ... ∨ ¬xn) is described but not fully formalized (when and how it is learned/added, interaction with existing learned clauses, treatment of unit propagation and backjumping). Provide a clear algorithmic specification and discuss interactions with core CDCL features (clause learning, restarts, variable selection heuristics).

3) Experimental evaluation lacks detail and rigor. The text refers to tables and figures but does not include their contents in the provided excerpt. Important experimental details are missing or under-specified: exact benchmark lists, number of instances, time/memory limits (time limit is mentioned in passing), parameter settings, reproducibility information, and statistical treatment. Comparison is limited: the only baseline is blocking-clause enumeration implemented on MiniSAT; other known AllSAT approaches and state-of-the-art enumerators are not compared (authors mention plans to compare in future work). The paper should add more baselines, quantify the number of models enumerated, and report memory usage and wall-clock times in tables.

4) Claims are too strong without supporting data. For instance, the authors say BR-Enum outperforms blocking clause methods by several orders of magnitude; this should be supported by detailed aggregated results and discussion of variance across instances.

5) Presentation and clarity: the manuscript contains duplicated sentences and some formatting/typographic issues (likely OCR/compilation artifacts in the provided text). Some definitions and notation are standard but could be tightened. The flow around the proposed technique would benefit from a short illustrative example showing step-by-step how BR-Enum proceeds on a small formula and why it avoids adding many blocking clauses.

6) Relation to prior work: the literature review is somewhat superficial. There is mention of blocking-clause methods and a few references, but the authors should discuss more thoroughly existing AllSAT and #SAT enumeration techniques and how their approach compares conceptually. Also discuss complexity implications and when BR-Enum is expected to be advantageous (e.g., instances with huge numbers of models concentrated in local neighborhoods of the search tree).

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add a clear formal statement of the algorithm, with pseudocode, and a correctness proof (soundness: no spurious models; completeness: enumerates all models; termination). If a full proof is difficult, provide sufficient invariant and argumentation clarifying assumptions under which the method is correct.
- Clarify interactions with restarts, clause learning, and variable heuristics; discuss whether restarts are permanently disabled after the first model or re-enabled later, and why.
- Expand experiments: include more baselines (other AllSAT tools and enumerators), publish tables/plots showing per-instance results, number of models found, memory usage, and runtimes; evaluate sensitivity to solver parameters (VSIDS, decision order). Make benchmarks and code available for reproducibility.
- Add at least one worked example illustrating BR-Enum on a small CNF to demonstrate how it steers the search without blocking clauses.
- Clean the text: remove duplicates, correct typos, and ensure all references and tables are present.

Conclusion:
The paper proposes a simple, potentially useful modification to CDCL solvers aimed at efficient model enumeration. The idea is promising and the preliminary empirical claims are encouraging. However, the current manuscript lacks essential formal justification of correctness and a rigorous, reproducible experimental evaluation. I recommend revision: strengthen the theoretical presentation, add more comprehensive experiments and comparisons, and improve the clarity and formatting. With these improvements the work could make a useful contribution to practical AllSAT methods.
